reason: Both answers correctly state exactly-once delivery is impossible, distinguish delivery from processing, and recommend at-least-once + idempotent processing as the standard approach — no disqualifying errors in either. A goes further with concrete, decision-relevant specifics: an example contrasting safe idempotent ops vs. dangerous ones, and importantly, caveats pinpointing exactly where teammates commonly get confused (Kafka EOS only holds Kafka-to-Kafka, SQS FIFO dedup is producer-side only within a window, dedup tables need retention limits). Since the scenario involves convincing a teammate who insists on a specific config, these precise, falsifiable caveats are highly actionable rather than padding. B is accurate and concise, and it names the outbox pattern explicitly, but its coverage of the "exactly-once" marketing claims is more generic/compressed. The extra length in A is doing real work, not just restating points, so it wins narrowly on usefulness.
